nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal floodplain pools, temporary ponds and marshes of the Argentine Pampas; ephemeral lowland water bodies that dry out completely during the austral summer dry season

decorPreferences: peat substrate or peat moss layer (minimum 5–10 cm depth) for egg deposition
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
dense surface planting (floating plants such as water lettuce or frogbit)
dark substrate to reduce fish stress
minimal or no filtration to avoid disturbing substrate; air-powered sponge filter preferred

diet.notes: The Argentine Pearl Fish is an obligate carnivore that tends to prefer live and frozen foods such as bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, and small aquatic invertebrates. Dry foods are often refused, though some specimens will accept high-quality micro-pellets or freeze-dried alternatives after a period of conditioning. Small portions offered once or twice daily are advisable to avoid water fouling in compact aquaria.

goodWith: small peaceful South American killifish of similar size

summary: The Argentine Pearl Fish is a short-lived annual killifish from the seasonal pools of the Argentine Pampas. Adults rarely survive beyond 12 months; the species perpetuates itself by burying eggs in the substrate, where they survive the dry season dormant and hatch when rains return. Cooler temperatures suit it better than most killifish.
careNotes: The Argentine Pearl Fish (Cynolebias whitei) is a true annual killifish endemic to the seasonal pools of the Argentine Pampas. Its entire wild life cycle completes in under 12 months: adults spawn into the muddy substrate of ephemeral pools, which dry out completely each year; the buried eggs survive desiccation via embryonic diapause (a state of suspended development) and hatch only when rains return. In the aquarium this annual biology is reproduced by housing a trio (1 male, 2 females) in a small species-only tank with a deep layer of peat moss or compressed peat as a spawning medium. Eggs are collected every 4–6 weeks by siphoning or removing the peat, which is then gently squeezed and stored slightly moist in a sealed plastic bag at 18–22°C for 10–16 weeks before being flooded with soft, slightly acidic water to trigger hatching. Water temperature should be kept cool (16–24°C) reflecting the temperate Argentine climate; unlike the African Nothobranchius killifish, this species tends to tolerate and benefit from temperatures well below 24°C. Water quality should be maintained carefully: peat filtration or blackwater extract helps simulate the tannin-rich, soft, mildly acidic native water. Males tend to be mildly territorial during spawning but rarely cause serious injury; keeping females at a 2:1 ratio with the male is generally advised to distribute attention. Adults are short-lived even under optimal conditions, and hobbyists should expect visible decline at 8–12 months of age regardless of husbandry. The genus Cynolebias has been progressively revised since the early 2000s, with numerous species reassigned to Austrolebias (the most species-rich of the split genera), Megalebias, Simpsonichthys, and others; C. whitei may be subject to further reclassification as taxonomic work in this group continues.
breedingNotes: The Argentine Pearl Fish is an egg-burying annual killifish that requires a dedicated peat-lined spawning setup. Condition a breeding pair on live or frozen foods for 1–2 weeks before introducing them to the spawning tank. Spawning occurs as the pair descend together into the peat substrate, releasing and fertilising eggs one to five at a time; this substrate-diving behaviour is characteristic of Cynolebias and related annual genera. Collect the peat every 4–6 weeks, squeeze out excess water until barely moist, and seal it in a labelled bag stored in the dark at 18–22°C. After 10–16 weeks of diapause, flood the peat with 2–3 cm of soft, slightly warm water (20–22°C); most fry tend to hatch within 24–48 hours. Feed fry micro-foods immediately: infusoria, microworms, and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii are all suitable. Fry generally grow rapidly and may reach sexual maturity within 6–8 weeks. Multiple diapause stages (DI, DII, and DIII) have been described in annual killifish eggs, and incubation duration may need adjustment if development appears slow. Brood sizes are typically 20–60 eggs per collection.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Cynolebias whitei
primary.notes: Primary source for adult size (males to 6–7 cm), temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Argentina, Entre Ríos province), habitat description, spawning behaviour, diapause egg care protocol, and diet. Also confirms family Rivulidae, order Cyprinodontiformes, and notes taxonomic uncertainty within the Cynolebias/Austrolebias complex.

additional.name: FishBase — Cynolebias whitei
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Rivulidae, Cyprinodontiformes), adult size (up to 7 cm TL), native range (La Plata basin, Argentina), and temperature tolerance. Confirms the species is listed under Cynolebias but notes synonymy/reclassification discussions linked to Austrolebias-group revisions by Costa 2002.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Killifish Care Guide
additional.notes: General annual killifish husbandry guide. Used to cross-check peat spawning protocol, egg diapause storage conditions, fry feeding recommendations, and carnivore diet guidance applicable to South American annual killifish including Cynolebias.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Annual killifish and diapause eggs
additional.notes: Trade-press feature covering annual killifish breeding methodology including peat storage, diapause phases (DI/DII/DIII), and hatching triggers. Supplemented egg collection and storage protocol in breedingNotes.

additional.name: Catalog of Fishes (Eschmeyer) via FishBase — Cynolebias
additional.notes: Nomenclatural reference confirming authorship (Myers, 1942) and current genus placement. Documents synonymy history relevant to the Cynolebias/Austrolebias taxonomic split and the ongoing revision of South American annual killifish genera.

fish.breedingNotes: Peat-substrate egg burier; eggs require 10–16 weeks dry diapause storage before flooding to trigger hatching. Fry are small and require live micro-foods. Accessible to killifish hobbyists but requires understanding of annual killifish diapause management.
